---
title: "Getting Started in R and RStudio"
pagetitle: "Slides for Getting Started in R and RStudio"
subtitle: "Parts 1 & 2 (4/30/26, 5/11/26)"
institute: "OHSU OCTRI-BERD"
author: "Jessica Minnier, Meike Niederhausen"
# date: "04/30/2026"
date-modified: "today"
format: 
  revealjs:
      incremental: false
      scrollable: true
      chalkboard: true
      theme: [sky_modified_smaller_font.scss]
      width:  1100 #default 1050
      height: 825  #default 700
      slide-number: true
      html-math-method: mathjax
      link-external-newwindow: true
  # html:
  #   link-external-newwindow: true
  #   toc: true
execute:
  echo: true
  freeze: auto  # re-render only when source changes
# editor: visual
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r}
#| label: "setup" 
#| include: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Load packages, this code must be run every time
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(gtsummary)
library(rstatix) # if you have issues with this, comment out using #
library(readxl)
library(janitor)
library(naniar)
library(writexl)

# set ggplot theme for slides 
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 22))
```


## (DONE) Workshop webpage

Please go to the webpage [https://ohsu-octri-berd.github.io/R_Intro_BERD_2026_04/](https://ohsu-octri-berd.github.io/R_Intro_BERD_2026_04/){target="_blank"} to access workshop materials.

## (DONE) Overview

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Part 1: 4/30/2026

-   Intro to R & RStudio
-   Quarto documents & code chunks
-   R packages
-   Data: loading, viewing
-   Summarizing data
    -   Quantitative
    -   Categorical
-   Data visualizations
    -   Quantitative: histograms, boxplots, scatterplots
    -   Categorical: barplots
-   Missing data summary
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Part 2: 5/11/2026

-   Data wrangling
    -   Sorting data
    -   Subsetting data (rows, columns)
    -   Creating new variables in a dataset
    -   Factor level variables
    -   Replacing missing values with another value
-   Some more data viz (stratifying figures)
-   Resources
:::
:::

# (DONE) Introduction to R

![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_welcome_R.png){fig-align="center"}

## (DONE) What is R?

::: columns
::: {.column width="70%"}
-   A programming language
-   Focus on statistical modeling and data analysis
    -   import data, manipulate data, run statistics, make plots
-   Useful for data science
-   Great visualizations
-   Also useful for most anything else you'd want to tell a computer to do
-   Interfaces with other languages i.e. python, C++, bash
-   Free and open source!
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
![](image/R_logo.png)
:::
:::

For the history and details: [Wikipedia](https://bit.ly/1efFmaY){target="_blank"}

-   an interpreted language (run it through a command line)
-   procedural programming with functions
-   Why "R"?? Scheme inspired S (invented at Bell Labs in 1976) which inspired R since 1st letters of original authors (**free and open source!** in 2000)

## (DONE) What is RStudio?

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
R is a programming language
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE) = an interface to use R (with perks!)
:::
:::

![[Modern Dive](https://moderndive.com/1-getting-started.html#r-rstudio){target="_blank"}](image/r_vs_rstudio_1.png){fig-align="center"}

## (DONE) Open RStudio on your computer (not R!)

![[Modern Dive](https://moderndive.com/1-getting-started.html#using-r-via-rstudio){target="_blank"}](image/01_md_r.png){fig-align="center"}

## (DONE) RStudio anatomy

![[Emma Rand](http://www-users.york.ac.uk/~er13/17C%20-%202018/pracs/01IntroductionToModuleAndRStudio.html#what_are_r_and_rstudio){target="_blank"}](image/RStudio_Anatomy.svg){fig-align="center"}

Read more about RStudio's layout in Section 3.4 of [Getting Used to R, RStudio, and R Markdown](https://ismayc.github.io/rbasics-book/3-rstudiobasics.html#rstudio-layout){target="_blank"} (Ismay and Kennedy 2021)

# Creating reproducible reports with Quarto

![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/r-packages-functions){target="_blank"}](image/horst_rmarkdown_wizards_quarto.png){fig-align="center"}

## Quarto = `.qmd` file = Code + text $\to$ html

`.qmd` files contain code + markdown syntax which can be "rendered" to other formats (html, pdf, Word, etc)

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
.qmd file ![](image/01_qmd_screenshot.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
html output\
![](image/default_quarto_html.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

# Basic Quarto example

![[Artwork from "Hello, Quarto" keynote by Julia Lowndes and Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, presented at RStudio Conference 2022. Illustrated by Allison Horst.](https://allisonhorst.com/cetinkaya-rundel-lowndes-quarto-keynote){target="_blank"}](image/horst_quarto_moon_penguins.png){fig-align="center"}

## 1. Create a Quarto file (`.qmd`)

**Two options:**

1.  click on File $\rightarrow$ New File $\rightarrow$ Quarto Document...$\rightarrow$ OK,
2.  or in upper left corner of RStudio click on ![](image/green_plus_create_file.png){width="70"} $\rightarrow$ ![](image/select_quarto_option.png){width="250"}

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Pop-up window selections:**

-   Enter a title and your name
-   Select `HTML` output format (default)
-   Engine: select `Knitr`
-   Editor: Select `Use visual markdown editor`
-   Click `Create`
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](image/01_qmd_screenshot_popup.png)
:::
:::

## 2. Create a Quarto file (`.qmd`)

-   After clicking on `Create`, you should then see the following in your editor window:

![](image/01_qmd_screenshot.png){fig-align="center"}

## 3. Save the Quarto file (`.qmd`)

-   **Save the file** by
    -   selecting `File -> Save`,
    -   or clicking on ![](image/01_Script_Save.png){width="33" height="24"} (towards the left above the scripting window),
    -   or keyboard shortcut
        -   PC: *Ctrl + s*
        -   Mac: *Command + s*
-   You will need to specify
    -   a **filename** to save the file as
        -   ALWAYS use **.qmd** as the filename extension for Quarto files
    -   the **folder** to save the file in

## 4. Create (render) html file

We create the html file by **rendering** the .qmd file.

Two options:

1.  click on the Render icon ![](image/render_icon.png) at the top of the editor window,
2.  or use keyboard shortcuts
    -   Mac: *Command+Shift+K*
    -   PC: *Ctrl+Shift+K*

-   A new window will open with the html output.
-   You will now see both .qmd and .html files in the folder where you saved the .qmd file.

::: callout-note
-   The template .qmd file that RStudio creates will render to an html file by default.
-   The output format can be changed to create a Word doc, pdf, slides, etc.
:::

## .qmd file vs. its html output

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
.qmd file ![](image/01_qmd_screenshot.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
html output\
![](image/default_quarto_html.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

# 3 types of Quarto content

1.  [*Code chunks*]{style="color:darkorange"}. <- [*What this workshop focuses on*]{style="color:purple"}
2.  Text, lists, images, tables, links (covered in Quarto workshop)
3.  YAML metadata (covered in Quarto workshop)

*To learn more about working with Quarto, check out our BERD workshop*  
[*Creating Professional Presentations and Websites using R/Quarto*](https://ohsu-octri-berd.github.io/Quarto_BERD_2025/){target="_blank"}.

![[Illustration by Alison Hill and Allison Horst, for RStudio.](https://allisonhorst.com/horst-hill-collaborations){target="_blank"}](image/horst_hedgehog_text_code.png){fig-align="center"}

## Code chunks

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
.qmd file ![](image/01_qmd_screenshot.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
html output\
![](image/default_quarto_html.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

## What does a code chunk look like?

An empty code chunk looks like this:

`Visual editor`

![](image/01_qmd_chunk_empty_visual.png)

`Source editor`

![](image/01_qmd_chunk_empty_source.png)

::: callout-important
Note that a code chunks start with ```` ```{r} ```` and ends with ```` ``` ````. Make sure there is no space before ```` ``` ````.
:::

## (DONE) Enter and run code (1/2)

-   **Type R code** inside code chunks
-   **Select code** you want to run, by
    -   placing the cursor in the line of code you want to run,
    -   ***or*** highlighting the code you want to run

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   **Run selected code** by
    -   clicking on the ![](image/01_Script_Run.png) button in the top right corner of the scripting window and choosing `Run Selected Line(s)`,
    -   or typing one of the following key combinations:

|     |                      |
|-----|----------------------|
| Mac | **ctrl + return**    |
| PC  | **command + return** |

-   *Where does the output appear?*
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](image/01_qmd_coding1a.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

## Enter and run code (2/2)

::: {style="font-size: 80%;"}
-   **Run all code** in a chunk by
    -   by clicking the play button in the top right corner of the chunk
-   The code output appears below the code chunk
:::

![](image/01_qmd_coding2.png){fig-align="center"}

::: callout-note
-   The output should also appear in the Console.
-   Settings can be changed so that the output appears only in the Console and not below the code chunk:
    -   Select {{< fa solid gear >}} (to right of Render button) and then *Chunk Output in Console*.
:::

## Create a code chunk

3 options to create a code chunk

1.  Click on ![](image/code_chunk_insert_icon.png) at top right of the editor window, or

2.  [**Keyboard shortcut**]{style="color:darkorange"}

|     |                        |
|-----|------------------------|
| Mac | *Command + Option + I* |
| PC  | *Ctrl + Alt + I*       |

3.  `Visual editor`: Select `Insert` -\> `Executable Cell` -\> `R`

![](image/code_chunk_insert_visual.png)

# (DONE)R packages: installing & loading

![](image/tidyverse_celestial.jpg){fig-align="center"}

## (DONE) What are R Packages?

<div style="text-align: center;">
A good analogy for R packages is that they\
are like apps you can download onto a smart phone
</div>

![[ModernDive Figure 1.4](https://moderndive.com/1-getting-started.html#packages){target="_blank"}](image/R_vs_R_packages.png){fig-align="center"}


## (DONE) Installing packages

-   Packages contain additional functions and data

Two options to install packages:

1.  `install.packages()` *or*
2.  The "Packages" tab in Files/Plots/Packages/Help/Viewer window

```{r}
#| eval: false

install.packages("janitor")   # only do this ONCE, use quotes
```

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   **Only install packages once** *(unless you want to update them)*
-   Installed from [Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN)](https://cran.r-project.org/){target="_blank"} = package mothership
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](image/screenshot_packages_window.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

## (DONE) Packages used in this workshop


::: {.columns}
                                        
::: {.column width="62%"}
-   **{tidyverse}**
    -   A collection of many commonly used packages
    -   **[*Core* {tidyverse}]{style="color:purple"}**: most commonly used {tidyverse} packages
        -   These are automatically get loaded when {tidyverse} is loaded
        -   Packages listed in [purple]{style="color:purple"} below are a part of the core
        -   [Complete list of core {tidyverse} packages](https://tidyverse.tidyverse.org/#usage){target="_blank"})
    -   **[*Additional* {tidyverse}]{style="color:green"}** packages
        -   The [additional packages](https://tidyverse.tidyverse.org/#packages){target="_blank"} are installed when the {tidyverse} package is installed, but they are not loaded when the {tidyverse} package is loaded. 
        -   They need to be loaded individually to use.
        -   Packages listed in [green]{style="color:green"} below are additional {tidyverse} packages

<!-- ![](image/tidyverse_celestial.jpg){fig-align="center"} -->
:::
       
::: {.column width="8%"}

:::
::: {.column width="30%"}

-   For **loading** datasets:

    -   [{readr}]{style="color:purple"} 
    -   [{readxl}]{style="color:green"}

-   For **summarizing** and **visualizing** data:

    -   {janitor}
    -   {gt}
    -   {rstatix}
    -   {gtsummary}
    -   {naniar}
    -   [{ggplot2}]{style="color:purple"} 

-   for **wrangling** data

    -   [{dplyr}, {tidyr}]{style="color:purple"} 

:::
                                          
:::




## (DONE) Install packages used in this workshop

-   Run the code below to install packages:

```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
install.packages("tidyverse")  # this takes a while to install
install.packages("writexl")
install.packages("rstatix") 
install.packages("janitor")
install.packages("naniar")
install.packages("gt")
install.packages("gtsummary")
```

-   It is **VERY IMPORTANT** that you install these packages in order to run the code for the workshop!

## (DONE) Load packages with `library()` function

-   Use the `library()` function to load each required package.
-   Packages need to be loaded *every* time you open Rstudio.
    -   **The code to load packages MUST be in your qmd file.**
    -   *Tip: at the top of your qmd file, create a chunk that loads all of the R packages you want to use in that file.*

```{r}
library(janitor)    # run this every time you open Rstudio
```

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Check to see whether a package has been loaded:
    -   Go to Packages tab
    -   If box to left of package name is checked off, then it's loaded
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](image/screenshot_packages_window.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::

## (DONE) Load packages used in this workshop

-   Run the code below in your R session

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(writexl)
library(rstatix)
library(janitor)
library(naniar)
library(gt)
library(gtsummary)
library(readxl)  # part of tidyverse, but not the tidyverse core
```

::: callout-note
-   Note that the package names do not need quotes around them when using the `library()` function, but they are required when using the `install.packages()` function.
:::

# Data basics in R

![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/r-packages-functions){target="_blank"}](image/horst_data_cowboy.png){fig-align="center"}

## Dataset example (toy data)



::: {style="font-size: 110%;"}
-   [**Use Excel to open the dataset `toy_data.xlsx` that is located in the data folder.**]{style="color:darkorange"}

-   Terminology

    -   Rows are usually **observations** (i.e. a person's measurements)
    -   Columns are usually **variables** or **measures**

-   How many observations are in this dataset?

-   What are the variable types in this dataset?
:::

## Import data into R



::: {style="font-size: 110%;"}
-   We can import data from many file types, including .csv, .txt., .xlsx, plus SAS or Stata files

-   Once imported, R typically stores data as [**data frames**]{style="color:darkorange"}, or [**tibbles**]{style="color:darkorange"} if using the `{tidyverse}` package.

    -   These are examples of what we call [**object types**]{style="color:darkorange"} in R.
    -   For our purposes, these are essentially the same, and we will often use the terms interchangeably.
    -   These workshop slides will usually use the term **dataset** to generally refer to either a data frame or tibble.
    
:::

## Load data from an .xlsx (Excel) file: **point & click option**

**SAVE THE CODE after using the point & click option**

::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
-   In RStudio's Files window, open the `data` folder and
    -   then click on the dataset `toy_data.xlsx`.
-   Select the `Import Dataset...` option,
    -   Import Options: Type in `NA` in the "NA:" box, and
    -   then click on the `Import` button on the bottom right of the pop-up window.
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
![](image/load_data_point_click.png){fig-align="center"} ![](image/load_data_point_click_NA.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

-   **Copy the code from the console used to load the file into your script** (omitting the + sign):

```{r}
toy_data <- read_excel("data/toy_data.xlsx", na = "NA")
```
:::

## Load data from an .xlsx (Excel) file: **code option**

-   The point and click option used the `read_excel` function from the `readxl` package to load the xlsx file.
-   If comfortable with coding and the data, we can skip the point and click and directly write the code ourselves.
-   When loading the dataset, we need to specify where the xlsx file is located, which in this case is in the folder called `data`.
-   No matter which option we use, the [**code to load the data MUST be in your qmd**]{style="color:darkorange"}.

```{r}
# library(readxl) # commented out since loaded above already
toy_data <- read_excel("data/toy_data.xlsx", na = "NA")
```

-   This code assigns the output of the `read_excel()` function (data) into an object named `toy_data`
-   The [`<-` is the assignment operator]{style="color:green"}
-   After loading, the name of the dataset object `toy_data` will be in your Environment tab
-   To learn more about loading in Excel files, read the [readxl intro vignette](https://readxl.tidyverse.org/){target="_blank"}
-   Also check out the readxl cheatsheet ([this will download a pdf](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/main/data-import.pdf){target="_blank"}).

## View the `toy_data` dataset

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Run the `View` function below within RStudio.

```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
View(toy_data)
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
-   A new tab in the scripting window will appear with the `toy_data` dataset.
-   Note that the code chunk in the qmd file specifies `#| eval: FALSE` so that this code is not run while rendering the qmd file.
:::
:::

![](image/View_toy_data.png){fig-align="center"}

## What are the variable "types"?



| R variable type  | Description                                          |
|------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| `dbl`: double    | numbers; also `num` or `int` for numbers or integers |
| `chr`: character | text, "strings"                                      |
| `fct`: factor    | categorical variables stored with levels (groups)    |
| `lgl`: logical   | boolean (TRUE, FALSE)                                |



```{r}
glimpse(toy_data)
```

## Getting to know the dataset

### How many rows and columns?

```{r}
# dim is for dimensions
# Output: # of rows, # of columns
dim(toy_data)   

nrow(toy_data)

ncol(toy_data)
```

### What are the variable names?

```{r}
names(toy_data)
```

# The Youth Risk Behavior Surveillance System (YRBSS)

Data from the CDC's [Youth Risk Behavior Surveillance System (YRBSS)](https://www.cdc.gov/healthyyouth/data/yrbs/index.htm){target="_blank"}

-   complex survey data
-   national school-based survey
    -   conducted by CDC and state, territorial, and local education and health agencies and tribal governments
-   monitors six categories of health-related behaviors
    -   that contribute to the leading causes of death and disability among youth and adults
    -   including alcohol & drug use, unhealthy & dangerous behaviors, sexuality, and physical activity
    -   see [Questionnaires](https://www.cdc.gov/healthyyouth/data/yrbs/questionnaires.htm){target="_blank"}
-   the data in `yrbss_demo.xlsx` are a subset of data in the R package [`yrbss`](https://github.com/hadley/yrbss){target="_blank"}, which includes YRBSS from 1991-2013

# Practice time! (part 1)

Open the Excel file `yrbss_demo.xlsx` in the *data* *folder* to familiarize yourself with it.

Use R code to answer the following questions.

1.  Load the dataset `yrbss_demo.xlsx`. Make sure the name of the loaded data is `yrbss_data`.
2.  What are the column names?
3.  How many rows and columns are in `yrbss_data`?
4.  Which variables are numeric (quantitative)?
5.  Which variables are character?

# Load the YRBSS data

-   Open the Excel file `yrbss_demo.xlsx` in the *data* *folder* to familiarize yourself with it.
-   This is a larger version of the toy data above, but it is still a subset of the full data.

```{r}
# library(readxl)
# note we are saving the data from the Excel file in our environment 
# with the object name yrbss_data
yrbss_data <- read_excel("data/yrbss_demo.xlsx")
```

First look at the data:

```{r}
glimpse(yrbss_data)
```

-   After loading the dataset, you will see `yrbss_data` in the Data list of the Environment window.

## View the `YRBSS` dataset

-   Run the `View` function below within RStudio.
    -   A new tab in the scripting window will appear with the `yrbss_data` dataset.
-   Note that the code chunk specifies `#| eval: FALSE` so that this code is not run while rendering the .qmd file.

```{r}
#| eval: FALSE

View(yrbss_data)
```

First 20 rows shown below:

![](image/View_yrbss_data.png){fig-align="center"}

# Summarizing data and `|>`

-   Often we want to summarize columns of data
-   Quantitative data and categorical/text data will be summarized differently
-   We also introduce the [pipe symbol]{style="color:darkorange"} `|>`, which is used to string together functions

The [pipe is read "and then"]{style="color:darkorange"} and tells R the next function will be applied to the data on the left side of the pipe:

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  head() # shows top rows
```

# Summarizing quantitative data


## `get_summary_stats()` from the `{rstatix}` package

-   The **`{rstatix}` package** has a useful function called `get_summary_stats()` for quick statistics of quantitative data

```{r}
# library(rstatix)

yrbss_data |> 
  get_summary_stats(
    bmi, stweight,
    type = "common"
    ) 
```

-   The `type` options are
    -   `type = c("full", "common", "robust", "five_number", "mean_sd", "mean_se", "mean_ci", "median_iqr", "median_mad", "quantile", "mean", "median", "min", "max")`

## Stratified summaries (and more on `|>`)

-   Now let's **summarize the BMI and weight variables by a strata subgroup**.
-   We do this with the `group_by()` function
-   Notice that we now have two `|>`:
    -   `data |> group_by(...) |> get_summary_stats(...)`
    -   With the pipe symbol `|>` we can **string together many functions** in the order we want them applied. Example:
        -   first tell R what dataset to use,

        -   then have R group (stratify) the data by `sex`,

        -   and then calculate summary statistics

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(sex) |>
  get_summary_stats(
    bmi, stweight,
    type = "common"
    )
```

## Stratify by 2 variables

-   We can specify more than one variable within `group_by()` to stratify the summaries by more variables

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(sex, grade) |>
  get_summary_stats(
    bmi, stweight,
    type = "common"
    )
```

## Prettier table of output

-   Below we add on `gt()` at the end to make the table prettier.
    -   Make sure to check this out in the html output.
-   The `gt()` function is from the [`{gt}` package](https://gt.rstudio.com/){target="_blank"}

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(sex, grade) |>
  get_summary_stats(
    bmi, stweight,
    type = "common"
    ) |>
  gt()
```

## `summarize()` function

-   Another option for summarizing numeric data is the `summarize()` function
-   This is helpful if you want to create *customized summaries*
-   See [examples from BSTA 526](https://niederhausen.github.io/BSTA_526_W26/part4/part_04_b526.html#summarize-data){target="_blank"} on how to use `summarize()`

Below are brief examples to give you an idea of how `summarize()` works:

-   Simple `summarize()` example 
    -   one variable, one summary statistic, one grouping variable

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(grade) |>
  summarize(mean = mean(bmi, na.rm = TRUE)) |> 
  gt()
```

-   Advanced `summarize()` example 
    -   two variables, two summary statistics, two grouping variables

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  group_by(sex, grade) |>
  summarize(across(.cols = c(bmi, stweight), 
                   .fns = list(
                     mean = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
                     sd = ~ sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
                   ))) |> 
  gt()
```




# Two pipes in R: `|>` vs `%>%`

![](image/captchas_pipe_wickham.png){fig-align="center" width="40%"} 

[Image from [https://www.linkedin.com/posts/hadleywickham_rstats-activity-7435450431968919552-hY19](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/hadleywickham_rstats-activity-7435450431968919552-hY19){target="_blank"}]{.smaller}

## Two pipes in R: `|>` vs `%>%`

- Both pipes are used to write code from **left to right** 
    -   by passing the result of one step into the next. 


::: {.panel-tabset}
### Background

- The newer **base R pipe `|>`** was introduced in 2021 with the release of R 4.1.0.
- The original **magrittr pipe `%>%`** 
    -   comes from the {magrittr} package and 
    -   became popular through the {tidyverse}.

<br>

-   Using the **base R pipe `|>`** does *not* require loading the {tidyverse} package (which includes {magrittr}),
    -   while using the **magrittr pipe `%>%`** does require {tidyverse}.

### Similarities

-   They work the same when the left-hand side becomes the **first argument** of the next function.
-   Example: 

::: {.columns}
                                        
::: {.column width="50%"}

Using `|>`
```{r}
yrbss_data |> dim()
```

:::
                                          
::: {.column width="50%"}
Using `%>%`
```{r}
yrbss_data %>% dim()
```

:::
:::



### Differences

*When do `|>` and `%>%` behave differently?*

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### [Simple example]{style="color:purple"}

::: {.columns}
                                        
::: {.column width="50%"}

- `|>` is **stricter** and expects an actual function call on the right

```{r}
yrbss_data |> dim()
```

```{r}
#| error: true
yrbss_data |> dim
```

-   `yrbss_data |> dim()` works, but  `yrbss_data |> dim` does not: 
:::
                                          
::: {.column width="50%"}
With `%>%`, we can omit the `()` after the function name:
```{r}
yrbss_data %>% dim()
yrbss_data %>% dim
```

:::
:::

#### [Advanced example]{style="color:purple"}

-   Code below runs a linear regression model with dependent variable `bmi` and independent variable `age`.

- Using `%>%`: 
    -   note that for the data argument a `.` is used as the placeholder for the dataset `yrbss_data` since it's already being "fed into" the model.

```{r}
yrbss_data %>% lm(bmi ~ age, data = .)
```

- Using `|>`: 
-   Using a `.` as as a placeholder for the data does not work with the newer base R `|>`:

```{r}
#| error: true
yrbss_data |>  lm(bmi ~ age, data = .)
```

-   Starting with R 4.3.0, we can use a placeholder but it's an underscore`_`: 

```{r}
yrbss_data |>  lm(bmi ~ age, data = _)
```

#### [More advanced examples]{style="color:purple"}

See `Learn more` tab above for resources with more examples.

:::

### For this workshop

We will use `|>` because 

-   it is built into R, 
-   requires no extra package, and 
-   works well for most {tidyverse} code you will write as beginners

Our older workshops and many reference materials use `%>%`. 


### Learn more

-   [Differences between the base R and magrittr pipes](https://tidyverse.org/blog/2023/04/base-vs-magrittr-pipe/){target="_blank"} - Tidyverse blog post by Hadley Wickham
- [Discussion of differences on Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/67633022/what-are-the-differences-between-rs-native-pipe-and-the-magrittr-pipe){target="_blank"}
- [R 4.1.0 pipe overview](https://www.jumpingrivers.com/blog/new-features-r410-pipe-anonymous-functions/){target="_blank"}
-   [Comparing pipes: Base-R |> vs {magrittr} %>%](https://albert-rapp.de/posts/31_pipes_compared/31_pipes_compared){target="_blank"} 
    -   this resource includes examples and 
    -   also how to change the "default" pipe in RStudio from `%>%` to `|>` (keyboard shortcut will use `|>`)
:::





# Summarizing categorical data: frequency tables

-   We will be creating frequency tables using the **`tabyl()` function from the `{janitor}` package** (loaded above).

## Frequency `tabyl()` for one variable

Default table:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  tabyl(age)  
```

Make the output prettier using `adorn_` options and adding on `gt()`:

::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}
With adornments:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  tabyl(age)   |>
  adorn_totals("row") |> 
  adorn_pct_formatting(digits=2)
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
With adornments & `gt()`:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  tabyl(age)   |>
  adorn_totals("row") |> 
  adorn_pct_formatting(digits=2) |> 
  gt() # from the {gt} package
```
:::
:::

## Contingency tables (two-way tables)

-   **Contingency tables** summarize data for two categorical variables
    -   with each value in the table representing the number of times\
        a particular combination of outcomes occurs
-   **Row & column totals** added with `adorn_totals` option
    -   Can choose to include row and/or column totals

```{r}
#| output-location: column

# ages vs. grades:

yrbss_data |> 
  tabyl(age, grade) |>    
  adorn_totals(c("row", "col")) |> 
  adorn_title(placement = "combined") |> 
  gt()
```

## Contingency tables with percentages

-   How are the percentages calculated in the code below?
-   What else was added to the table, other than the percentages?

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  tabyl(age, grade) |>    
  adorn_totals(c("row")) |>
  adorn_percentages("row") |>   
  adorn_pct_formatting(digits=0) |>    
  adorn_ns() |> 
  gt()
```

## Stratified (3-way) tables

We can also add a third variable, which creates 2x2 tables stratified by the third variable:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  tabyl(age, grade, sex)
```

*Make sure to scroll down in the output box above to see all of the output*

## Resources for making nice tables

### More `tabyl` and `adorn_` options

-   [`tabyl`s vignette](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/janitor/vignettes/tabyls.html){target="_blank"} for more `tabyl` and `adorn_` options
-   `adorn_` `tabyl` [examples from BSTA 526](https://niederhausen.github.io/BSTA_526_W26/part7/part_07_b526.html#adorn-tabyls){target="_blank"}


### Making tables even nicer with additional `{gt}` options

-   See [examples from BSTA 526](https://niederhausen.github.io/BSTA_526_W26/part7/part_07_b526.html#add-gt-options-to-tabyl){target="_blank"} for adding `{gt}` options to tables
-   See the very useful [`{gt}` package vignettes](https://gt.rstudio.com/){target="_blank"} for more.

![Image from [https://gt.rstudio.com/](https://gt.rstudio.com/){target="_blank"}](image/gt_parts_of_a_table.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"} 




# Practice time! (part 2)

1.  Create a contingency table for `race4` and `race7`, with both percentages and counts. What is the difference between these two variables?
2.  Create a contingency table for `race4` and `race7`, with both percentages and counts, stratified by `grade`.
3.  Calculate the minimum, median, and maximum BMI and weights of participants in the YRBSS data.
4.  Calculate the minimum, median, and maximum BMI and weights of the participants in the YRBSS data, now stratified by age.

# Data visualization

-   This is a *very brief* introduction to creating plots using the `{ggplot2}` package.
    -   The `{ggplot2}` package gets loaded with the `{tidyverse}` and thus we do not need to load it separately.

::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
![[ggplot](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/index.html){target="_blank"}](image/ggplotlogo.png){fig-align="center" height="400"}
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/){target="_blank"}](image/ggplot2_masterpiece.png){fig-align="center" height="400"}
:::
:::

# Visualizing quantitative variables

-   What is the distribution shape of a quantitative variable?
-   Does the distribution shape of a quantitative variable change when stratified by a categorical variable?
-   Is there an association between two quantitative variables?

## Histograms

```{r}
ggplot(data = yrbss_data,    # specify the dataset
       # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used
       aes(x = bmi)) +  
  geom_histogram() # type of plot
```

## Boxplots

-   Recall that the whiskers' ends are the minimum and maximum values
-   The box is from the first to third quartiles, with the line inside the box at the median

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**BMI**:\
horizontal boxplot (specify `x = ...`)

```{r}
#| fig.height: 3
#| fig.width: 5

ggplot(data = yrbss_data,   # dataset
  # aes: specify vars being used
       aes(x = bmi)) +   
  geom_boxplot()   # type of plot
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Weight**: vertical boxplot (specify `y = ...`)

```{r}
#| fig.height: 3
#| fig.width: 5

ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
# use y instead of x in aes for vertical:
  aes(y = stweight)) + 
  geom_boxplot() 
```
:::
:::

## Stratified boxplots (plus axis labels & title!)

```{r}
ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(
         # horizontal boxplots for BMI (x=):
         x = bmi,
         # stratified vertically (y=) by age:
         y = age
         )) + 
  geom_boxplot() + 
  # Add on labels and a title:
  labs(x = "BMI", 
       y = "Age", 
       title =  "BMI distributions by age category"
       )  
```

## Add color using `fill`

-   Also remove the `NA` category with `drop_na()` (we will talk about this in later slides)
-   What's the difference in use of `fill=` here?

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| fig.height: 5
#| fig.width: 7
ggplot(
  data = yrbss_data |> drop_na(age), #NEW!
  aes(
    x = bmi,
    y = age,
    fill = age  # NEW!
  )) + 
  geom_boxplot() +
  # remove legend:
  theme(legend.position="none") 
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| fig.height: 5
#| fig.width: 7
ggplot(
  data = yrbss_data |> drop_na(age), #NEW!
  aes(
    x = bmi,
    y = age,
    fill = sex  # NEW!
  )) + 
  geom_boxplot() 
```
:::
:::

## Scatterplots

```{r}
ggplot(data = yrbss_data, # specify the dataset
       # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used
       aes(x = stweight,       
           y = bmi
           )) +  
  geom_point()    # type of plot     
```

## Scatterplot with color-coded dots

```{r}
ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       # aes = aesthetics: specify variables being used
       aes(x = stweight,      
           y = bmi,  
           color = sex,
           )) +   
  geom_point(alpha = .2) # alpha controls transparency of the points
```

## Color vs. fill vs. alpha



::: {style="font-size: 110%;"}
-   `color` (or `colour`) is used for lines and outlines
-   `fill` is used for interiors
-   Note: geom_point() shape default is a point with only an outline and no interior -\> *we used color and not fill*



-   `alpha` specifies the opacity / transparency
    -   Values range from 0 to 1
    -   lower alphas are more transparent



-   See ggplot webpage [Colour related aesthetics: colour, fill, and alpha](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/aes_colour_fill_alpha.html){target="_blank"} for more information
:::

# Visualizing categorical variables

## Barplots

By default, barplots display counts (frequencies) on the vertical axis:

```{r}
#| fig.width: 10
ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(x = age)) +
  geom_bar() + # specify plot type 
  
  # angle the x-axis labels so that they are not overlapping
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = -45, hjust = 0))
```

## Barplots with percents

To show percents, the code is more complicated.

```{r}
#| fig.width: 10
ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(x = age)) +
  # specify aesthetics within the barplot to show proportions
  geom_bar(aes(y = after_stat(prop), group = 1)) + 
  
  # Next line converts y-axis labels to percentages instead of proportions
  scale_y_continuous(labels =  scales::percent_format()) +
  # "dodge" the x-axis labels
  scale_x_discrete(guide = guide_axis(n.dodge = 2)) +
  labs(y = "Percent") # default label is prop

```

## Barplots with 2 variables: segmented bar plots

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
Stacked bars showing counts:

```{r}
#| fig.width: 10
ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(y = age,
           # specify stacking variable: 
           fill = grade)) +
  geom_bar()
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
For proportions within each bar:\
(added `position = "fill"` within `geom_bar()`)

```{r}
#| fig.width: 10
ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(y = age,
           # specify stacking variable:
           fill = grade)) + 
  geom_bar(position = "fill") 
```
:::
:::

-   Note that by using `y = age` instead of `x = age`, 
    -   the bars of the figure are now horizontal instead of vertical.
-   With the horizontal bars, we can see the long labels for each category.    

## Side-by-side barplots (dodged)

```{r}
#| fig.width: 10
ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(x = age,
           # specify variable for colors 
           fill = grade)) + 
  
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +  # make dodged bars
  
  # angle the x-axis labels so that they are not overlapping
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = -15, hjust = 0))
```

## Saving a ggplot as an image file

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
First, create a plot and save it as an R object:

```{r}
myplot <- ggplot(data = yrbss_data, 
       aes(x = age,
           # specify variable for colors 
           fill = grade)) + 
  geom_bar() +
  # angle the x-axis labels 
  #so that they are not overlapping
  theme(
    axis.text.x = 
      element_text(angle = -15, hjust = 0))

myplot
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
Save the plot as a pdf (or "jpeg", "tiff", "png", etc.)

```{r}
ggsave(
  filename = "output/myplot.pdf",
  plot = myplot,
  device = "pdf",
  width = 5,
  height = 3,
  units = "in",
  dpi = 200)
```

Can specify the dpi when saving, many journals have dpi requirements.

See [`ggsave` webpage]{https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/ggsave.html} for more details.


:::
:::

## \* More ggplot topics



-   Change the [**theme**](https://ggplot2-book.org/themes){target="_blank"} of the plot
    -   This includes the color scheme, text font and size, and other options.
    -   There are many existing themes to choose from.



-   Arrange multiple plots as one
    -   [patchwork](https://patchwork.data-imaginist.com/){target="_blank"} package
    -   Claus O. Wilke's [cowplot](https://wilkelab.org/cowplot/articles/index.html){target="_blank"} package
    -   [Shared legend for all plots!](https://wilkelab.org/cowplot/articles/shared_legends.html){target="_blank"}

## \* More ggplot workshop slides



-   These slides are heavily adapted from R workshops
    -   presented together with Jessica Minnier, PhD
    -   sponsored by *OCTRI Biostatistics, Epidemiology, Research & Design (BERD)*:



-   **Data Visualization with R and ggplot2** 2020/05/20
    -   html slides: [bit.ly/berd_ggplot](https://jminnier-berd-r-courses.netlify.app/04-ggplot/04_ggplot_slides.html){target="_blank"}
    -   pdf slides: [bit.ly/berd_ggplot_pdf](https://jminnier-berd-r-courses.netlify.app/04-ggplot/04_ggplot_slides.pdf){target="_blank"}
    -   [recording](https://echo360.org/media/34465dc2-ae46-4508-b5a7-b0fee860682e/public){target="_blank"} of the presentation

# Missing data summary & visualization

-   I *highly recommend* working through the tutorial **[Exploring missing values in `naniar`](https://allisonhorst.shinyapps.io/missingexplorer/).**
-   These slides have some highlights of tools presented in the tutorial

Load the `{naniar}` package if you haven't already:

```{r}
library(naniar)
```

![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/r-packages-functions){target="_blank"}](image/horst_naniar.png){fig-align="center"}

## Missingness statistics

Total number of missing values in dataset:

```{r}
n_miss(yrbss_data)
```

Proportion of missing values in dataset:

```{r}
prop_miss(yrbss_data)
```

Missingness by each variable:

```{r}
miss_var_summary(yrbss_data) |> gt()
```

## Visualizing missing data across dataset

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   This plot helps visualize whether
    -   there are variables with a lot of missing values and/or
    -   participants with a lot of missing values
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Each row is an observation
-   Columns are for the different variables in the dataset
-   Black = missing, grey = not missing
:::
:::

```{r}
vis_miss(yrbss_data) 
```

## Missingness heatmap by group

-   This plot is useful in comparing missingness rates stratified by a variable
-   Specify the variable to stratify the missingness heatmap by in the `fct =` option

Example: Percent missingness stratified by age 

```{r}
gg_miss_fct(yrbss_data, fct = age) 
```

## Missingness intersections (UpSet plot)

-   UpSet plots are great for finding sets of variables that have a lot of missingess.

```{r}
gg_miss_upset(yrbss_data, nsets = 10) 
```

# Practice time! (part 3)

1.  Create a histogram for the `stweight` variable. Describe the distribution shape.
2.  What happens if you change `x =` to `y =` in the histogram code?
3.  Create boxplots of `stweight` stratified by sex.
4.  Create a barplot of race4 categories with the y axis showing percentages.
5.  Visualize the missing data across the YRBSS subset dataset we created `toy_data` using `vis_miss()`. What missingness patterns do you see?

# Data wrangling

![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/r-packages-functions){target="_blank"}](image/horst_data_cowboy.png){fig-align="center"}

## **Sorting** datasets using `arrange()`

`arrange()` is a function that lets us **sort** a dataset by a specified variable.

-   By default, it sorts in *ascending* order:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  arrange(record)
```

-   To sort by descending order, you need to wrap the variable in the `desc()` function:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  arrange(desc(record))
```

## Sorting datasets by multiple variables

-   You can also arrange by multiple variables.
    -   The code below sorts by `age` *first*, and then within `age`, we sort by `record`.

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  arrange(age, record)
```

-   Note that order of variables in `arrange()` matters!

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  arrange(record, age)
```

## `arrange()` after `count()`-ing categorical data

-   Another example of when `arrange()` is useful
-   We've used `tabyl()` to see what values exist in a categorical variable.

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
-   We can also use `count()` to count all of the unique values for a categorical variable.
-   This will also show `NA` category, which is the special missing variable type:

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  count(age)
```
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
-   Sometimes it helps to arrange by that category if you have many categories to view.
-   This can also alert us if there are categorical coding mistakes (such as misspellings) in our data.

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  count(age) |>
  arrange(n)
```
:::
:::

## Saving changes we make to a dataset

-   Note that we have not changed the order of the `yrbss_data` dataset after arranging:

```{r}
head(yrbss_data)
```

-   We need to *save our work* by re-assigning the sorted/arranged dataset as `yrbss_data` with the `<-` operator:

```{r}
yrbss_data <- yrbss_data |> arrange(desc(record))

yrbss_data
```

# Subsetting data

![](image/datawrangle_cheatsheet_subset_rows.png){fig-align="center"}

![[data transformation cheatsheet](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/raw/master/data-transformation.pdf){target="_blank"}](image/datawrangle_cheatsheet_subset_cols.png){fig-align="center"}

# Subset rows: `filter()`ing data

![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_dplyr_filter.jpg){fig-align="center"}

## `filter()`ing quantitative data

`filter()` is an extremely powerful function. It lets us **subset our data** according to specific criteria.

-   Let's filter on a `numeric` variable, `bmi`: all rows with `bmi` values greater than 40

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter(bmi > 40)
```

-   Filter to only include all rows with `bmi` values equal to 13.9

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter(bmi == 13.9)
```

-   There are no rows with `bmi` values equal to 13.9, but `bmi` was rounded to 4 decimal places:

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter(bmi == 13.9229)
```

-   [***Note the double equal signs!***]{style="color:darkorange"} 
    -   `==` is a *test* of whether two things are equal, 
    -   it is not an "assignment" (i.e. `x = 5` assigns the value 5 to the object `x`).





## `filter()`ing cateogrical data

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   To filter on a categorical variable, we need to know the values (levels) of the variable:
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
yrbss_data |> tabyl(grade)
```
:::
:::

-   [***Note the double equal signs!***]{style="color:darkorange"} 
    -   `==` is a *test* of whether two things are equal, 
    -   it is not an "assignment" (i.e. `x = 5` assigns the value 5 to the object `x`).

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter(grade == "10th") # We must include " " around the value for categorical variables
```

-   If we want everything except 10th grade, we use the ["not equal" sign]{style="color:darkorange"} `!=`

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter(grade != "10th")
```

## Filtering requires a little logic

-   We can chain multiple criteria using the [`&` (AND)]{style="color:darkorange"} or [`|` (OR) operators]{style="color:darkorange"}.
    -   But we need to review a little logic before we do this.



-   If we wanted to return participants who identified as
    -   `Female` **and** are in 10th grade,
    -   we would use an `&` to chain these criteria together:

```         
sex == "Female" & grade == "10th"
```



-   Note that *SPELLING AND CASE MATTER*!
    -   You must write the name of the column exactly and
    -   also the value (i.e. "Female" not "female") exactly.

## Filtering with `&` (AND) criteria

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter(sex == "Female" & 
           grade == "10th")

```

-   Note that we could also use the comma in this case:

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter(sex == "Female",    # comma is equivalent to & within filter()
         grade == "10th")

```

## Filtering with `|` (OR) criteria

If we wanted patients who were

`Female` [**or**]{style="color:darkorange"} in 10th grade

we would use a `|` to chain these criteria together.

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter(sex == "Female" |
           grade == "10th")
```

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Think about it:

Which of the AND vs. OR code blocks will return a larger number of participants?
:::

## More filter examples

-   See [this slide](https://jminnier-berd-r-courses.netlify.app/02-data-wrangling-tidyverse/02_data_wrangling_slides_part1.html#27){target="_blank"} from a previous BERD workshop.

-   Also read the [filter section](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/data-transform.html#rows){target="_blank"} in the "Data Transformation" chapter from *R for Data Science (2e)*.

<br>

### More about comparison and logical operators

Click [here](https://www.datamentor.io/r-programming/operator/){target="_blank"} for a useful reference for all the different operators (both logical and comparison) that you can use.

<br>

### New since Feb 2026: `filter_out()`

-   `filter_out()` requires `{dplyr}` 1.2.0 (or later version)

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  filter_out(sex == "Female") |> 
  tabyl(sex) # check 
```


## Filtering out missing values using `drop_na()` (1/2)

-   We may want to remove rows that have missing data, which are coded as `NA`.

-   See the [`drop_na()`](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/reference/drop_na.html){target="_blank"} reference for examples (`?drop_na`).



-   First look at rates of missingness using the `naniar` package:

```{r}
naniar::miss_var_summary(yrbss_data)
```

## Filtering out missing values using `drop_na()` (2/2)

*Pay attention to the number of rows for each of these results*

-   Remove missingness in one column:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> drop_na(race7)
```

-   Remove missingness in two columns:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> drop_na(race7, bmi)
```

-   Remove all missingness:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> drop_na()
```

## A warning about `filter()`: check for NA

-   Suppose we want to remove the `10th` grade category.
-   However this column has missingness in it as we can see with `tabyl()`.
-   When we try to filter using `!="10th"` we lose our `NAs`!
    -   That isn't really expected.
-   You need to account for this with an *or* `|` statement:

::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  tabyl(grade)
```

Lost the `NA`'s in the filtering below!

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  filter(grade != "10th") |>
  tabyl(grade)
```
:::

::: {.column width="55%"}
Updated code to keep the `NA`'s when filtering:

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  # add an | (OR) statement 
  # to keep in the NA values
  filter((grade != "10th") |
           is.na(grade)) |>
  tabyl(grade)
```
:::
:::

# Subset columns: `select()`ing data

![[data transformation cheatsheet](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/raw/master/data-transformation.pdf){target="_blank"}](image/datawrangle_cheatsheet_subset_cols.png){fig-align="center"}

## Selecting columns using `select()`

-   The `select()` function allows us to select variables or columns from our dataset:

::: {.panel-tabset}

### basic example

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  select(record, age, race4)

```

### remove columns

-   If we want to select everything *except* specified variable(s),
    -   we can use a `-` in front of the variable(s)

```{r}
yrbss_data |>
  select(-race4, -race7)

```

### consecutive columns

-   Select consecutive columns with `var_first:var_last`
-   Make sure you know the order of your columns so that this is correct

```{r}
yrbss_data |> select(age:grade)
```

### rearrange columns

-   `select()` and `everything()` are a useful combination for some quick rearranging of columns:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> select(record, bmi, everything())
```

-   There's also a function [`relocate()` to move around your column order](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/relocate.html){target="_blank"}.
-   The three options below all create the same results:

```{r}
yrbss_data |> relocate(record, bmi, everything()) |> names()
yrbss_data |> relocate(bmi, .after = record) |> names()
yrbss_data |> relocate(bmi, .before = age) |> names()
```

:::

## `{tidyselect}`

-   There are ways to select columns by "searching" for them.
-   These are called `{tidyselect}` helpers.
-   See more examples from the [{tidyselect} webpage](https://tidyselect.r-lib.org/reference/language.html){target="_blank"}.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### `contains`

-   For instance, you can see how this might be useful, where we select all columns where the column name includes the word "race":

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  select(contains("race"))
```

### `starts_with`, `ends_with`

-   Select all columns starting with "race":

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  select(starts_with("race")) |> 
  names()
```

-   Select all columns ending with "e":

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  select(ends_with("e")) |> 
  names()
```

### Select columns by data type

-   We can also select variables based on data types
-   For instance, `select(where(is.numeric))` selects all numeric columns
    -   (`where()` is a helper function that returns columns where the inside function is `TRUE`,
    -   in this case `is.numeric` returns the `TRUE`/`FALSE`)


```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
  select(where(is.numeric)) |> 
  names()

yrbss_data |> 
  select(where(is.character)) |> 
  names()

```


:::


## The difference between `filter()` and `select()`



-   One thing to keep in mind is that:
    -   `filter()` works on [rows]{style="color:darkorange"} (think FILTER in Excel!), and
    -   `select()` works on [columns]{style="color:darkorange"} (select your relevant variables)



Keep that in mind!

## Renaming with `rename()`

-   You can rename columns with `select()` but it's a bit easier to do this with the `rename()` function.

-   You just need to remember **`new_name = old_name`** ordering.

    -   One equal sign will suffice!

```{r}
yrbss_data |> rename(id = record,
                  race_cat = race4,
                  weight = stweight)
```



*Note that [we did not save the changes above]{style="color:darkorange"}, and thus we haven't actually renamed the variables in the dataset `yrbss_data`!*

## Saving our results as a file

-   Let's save some processed data in the `output/` directory.
-   We'll save it as an `xlsx` file so that we can open it in Excel.

```{r}
# chain together several data transformation steps with |>

# use <- to assign the new data to a different name to avoid overwriting the original dataset

processed_data <- yrbss_data |>   
  select(-race7) |>
  rename(id = record,
         race_cat = race4,
         weight = stweight) |>
  filter(sex == "Female")

glimpse(processed_data)
```

[Save]{style="color:darkorange"} the `processed_data`:

```{r}
write_xlsx(x = processed_data,
                path = "output/processed_data.xlsx")
```

# Practice time! (part 4)

Perform some subsetting functions on the `yrbss_data` data:

1.  Use `filter()` to select people who are in 9th or 10th grade and who have BMI less than 25. Save the resulting dataset as `yrbss_filtered`.

2.  Use `rename()` to rename 2 of the column names of `yrbss_filtered` to something more meaningful.

3.  Use `select()` to keep only the columns that denote record, sex, grade, bmi, and the 2 renamed columns.

4.  Arrange the data by bmi, starting with the highest value and ending with the least value.

5.  Save the resulting data set in the file `data/practice4_data.xslx`.



# Make new variables: `mutate()`

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_mutate.jpg){fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![[data transformation cheatsheet](https://github.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/raw/master/data-transformation.pdf){target="_blank"}](image/datawrangle_cheatsheet_makenewvars.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::



## `mutate()` - A confusing name, a powerful `{dplyr}` verb

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

-   You can use `mutate()` to
    -   transform existing columns (variables in your dataset) and/or
    -   add new columns to the dataset.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_mutate.jpg){fig-align="center" width="70%"}
:::
:::

Think of this like adding a formula in Excel to calculate the value of a new column based on previous columns. You can do lots of things such as:

-   subtract one column from another
-   convert the units of one column to new units (such as days to years)
-   change the capitalization of categories in a variable
-   recode a continuous variable to be a categorical one

## Calculate a new variable based on other variables with `mutate` 

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Example 1

-   Convert the weights to kg, assuming `stweight` is in lbs

```{r}
yrbss_data |> 
    mutate(weight_kg = stweight/2.205) |>
    glimpse()
```

:::{.callout-note}
-   Notice we use `=` inside mutate, not `==` or `<-`.
-   We are not making a logical operation, nor are we assigning something to an object name.
:::


:::{.callout-important}
Since we haven't assigned this new "mutated" dataset to an object name, our work hasn't been saved.

:::


### Example 2

1.   Convert the weights to kg, assuming `stweight` is in lbs (same as in Ex 1)
2.   Convert the weights to g, using the kg weights
3.   Create a new dataset (`yrbss_new`) with the new variables

```{r}
yrbss_new <- yrbss_data |> 
    mutate(weight_kg = stweight/2.205,
           weight_g = weight_kg*1000)

glimpse(yrbss_new)
```

-   We saved the new dataset as `yrbss_new`
    -   to avoid overwriting our other data set.
    -   Check out the environment tab to see the new dataset listed.
-   Notice how we can add multiple new columns within one `mutate()` function.
    -   We can also mutate using multiple columns.
    -   Here we have created two new weight variables in different scales
    -   In the next *Practice time*, you will make a height variable from `bmi` and `weight_kg`

### Examine new columns

Figure comparing the newly created `weight_kg` column to the original `stweight` column:

```{r}
ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = stweight, 
      y = weight_kg) +
  geom_point()
```

:::

## Categorical data (`factor`s) 

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Intro

-   One data type that we haven't yet looked at are `factor` variables
    -   [factors are how R represents **levels** of *categorical data*]{style="color:darkorange"}.
-   For the most part, you can use `character` and `factors` interchangeably for categorical data.
-   However, there are two main differences:
    -   `factor`s define the *permissible* values in a vector with an argument called `levels`.
    -   The levels define the order in which the permissible values are displayed, such as in a table or figure.
    -   The default order of levels (values) is in **alphanumeric** order.



### Make `grade` factor

-   Below we use the `factor()` function within a `mutate()` to transform `grade` from a `character` variable into a `factor` variable.

```{r}
class(yrbss_new$grade) # what's the data type of grade?
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade)

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> 
  #create a factor variable
  mutate(grade_fac1 = factor(grade)) 

# Checks:
class(yrbss_new$grade_fac1) # what's the data type of grade_fac1?
levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac1) # what are its levels?
# values are the same in the two grade variables:
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade, grade_fac1) |> adorn_title()
```

### Change order of levels

-   Use the `levels` argument within the `factor()` function

Example: Create `grade_fac2` variable as a factor version of grade with grades in "correct" order:

```{r}
yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> 
  #create a factor variable
  mutate(grade_fac2 = factor(
    grade, 
    levels = c("9th","10th","11th","12th")
    ))

# Checks:
class(yrbss_new$grade_fac2) # what's the data type of grade_fac1?
levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac2) # what are its levels?
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac2)

# values are the same in the two grade factor variables:
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac1, grade_fac2) |> adorn_title()
```

### Be careful! 

-   Recall that the `levels` of a factor are the *permissible values* in a `factor`.

::: callout-warning
*Spelling matters!*
:::

-   If we try to use a factor level that doesn't exist ("10" vs "10th"),
    -   we won't have any data in that category, and
    -   the non-specified levels ("10") get set to `NA` (missing).

```{r}
yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> 
  mutate(grade_fac3 = factor(
    grade, 
    levels = c("9th","10","11th","12th") # typo!!
    )) 

# check
levels(yrbss_new$grade_fac3) # what are its levels?
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac3)
# compare
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade_fac2, grade_fac3) |> adorn_title()
```


### Replace `grade` with factor version

-   Below we are doing something called *reassignment*:
    -   We’re taking the previous values of our variable (`grade`),
    -   doing something to them (making them a factor), and 
    -   then reassigning the variable `grade` to its new set of values.
-   *I don't recommend replacing a variable with new values until you are 100% positive that your code is correct!!*
    -   I can't compare the new `grade` values to the original ones anymore to check my work

```{r}
yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> 
  mutate(grade = factor(
    grade, 
    levels = c("9th","10th","11th","12th")
    )) 

# compare to grade_fac2 (which we determined to be correct)
yrbss_new |> tabyl(grade, grade_fac2) |> adorn_title()
```

### Remove extra variables

-   This step isn't necessary, but we don't need the columns `grade_fac1`, `grade_fac1-grade_fac3` anymore.

```{r}
names(yrbss_new)
yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |> select(-grade_fac1:-grade_fac3)
names(yrbss_new)
```


:::



# Practice time! (part 5)

1.  Create a new variable `height` that calculates height based on `bmi` and `weight_kg`. Note the formula for BMI is weight/height\^2 (weight is in kilograms and height is in meters). Create a scatterplot of `bmi` vs `height`.

2.  Create a new factor variable `sex_fac` that orders the values of `sex` as Male, Female.

# Make variables with `case_when()`

![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/dplyr_case_when.png){fig-align="center"}

## `case_when()`

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Intro

-   How can we dichotomize a continuous variable, such as BMI?
-   How can we combine categories of a variable, such as race?
-   How can we convert categories to numbers, such as sex?

These are all examples where `case_when()` can be used.

### Code

-   The code follows a **basic pattern** for each category inside `case_when()`:
    -   [*condition* \~ *category name*]{style="color:darkorange"}
-   The *left* side of the `~` is where we specify how to *define* the category.
-   The *right* side of the `~` is where we specify the *category name*.
-   Separate each "case" with a comma.

### Example 1

```{r}
yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex)
```


-   Convert `sex` to a new numeric variable called `female` whose values are
    -   1 when `sex` is Female, and
    -   0 when `sex` is Male

[*left side* defines the condition *\~* *right side* names the category]{style="color:darkorange"}

```{r}
yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(
    female = case_when(
      # when sex is Female, make female equal to 1 
      sex == "Female" ~ 1, # do not forget this comma!
      
      # when sex is Male, make female equal to 0
      sex == "Male" ~ 0
      )
    ) 

# checks
yrbss_new |> tabyl(female) # look at the categories
class(yrbss_new$female)
# compare female and sex variables
yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex, female) |> adorn_title()
```

-   How were the missing values (`NA`) handled?



### Example 2

-   Dichotomize the numeric variable `bmi` into two categories with 30 as the cutoff.

[*left side* defines the condition *\~* *right side* names the category]{style="color:darkorange"}

```{r}
yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(
    bmi30 = case_when(
      bmi < 30 ~ "<30", # don't forget a comma between cases
      bmi >= 30 ~ "30+"
      ) 
  ) 

# check
yrbss_new |> tabyl(bmi30)
class(yrbss_new$bmi30)

# check values were categorized correctly: (look at mins & maxes)
yrbss_new |> 
  group_by(bmi30) |> 
  get_summary_stats(bmi) |> 
  gt()

```


### Example 3

-   Recode `bmi` to have 4 levels:
    -   `<20` (not including exactly 20)
    -   `20-30` (not including exactly 30)
    -   `30+`
    -   `unknown` if missing

-   Code comments
    -   Notice that for the middle category we use an `&` statement similar to how we used logic in filter
    -   New: used `.default` option to specify what to assign in all other unspecified cases.

```{r }
yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(
    bmi3cat = case_when(
      bmi < 20 ~ "<20",
      (bmi >=20) & (bmi < 30) ~ "20-30",
      bmi >= 30 ~ "30+",
      .default = "unknown"  # apply this in all other cases
      ),
    bmi3cat = factor(bmi3cat) # make it factor after creating categories
    )
  
# check
yrbss_new |> tabyl(bmi3cat)
class(yrbss_new$bmi3cat)

# check values were categorized correctly: (look at mins & maxes)
yrbss_new |> 
  group_by(bmi3cat) |> 
  get_summary_stats(bmi) |> 
  gt()
```

:::


# Practice time! (part 6)

1.  Create a new variable called `grade_num` in `yrbss_new` that converts `grade` to numeric.

2.  Create a new variable called `race_cat` that relabels `race4` to have shorter race category names: W (White), H/L (Hispanic/Latino), B/AA (Black or African American), Other (All other races). Make `race_cat` a factor variable with levels ordered by the size of the group in descending order.

3.  Create a `tabyl` of the new variable `race_cat` and `race4` to check your work, including the ordering of the factors.


# Replace missing values with `replace_na()`

## `replace_na()`

-   Sometimes we want to fill in missing values with a certain value.
-   We can use the `replace_na()` function inside of `mutate()` to specify this.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Example 1

-   For example, suppose if the bmi variable is missing,
    -   we want to replace it with the average bmi across the entire data set.

```{r}
yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
  mutate(
    bmi_filled = replace_na(
      bmi,
      mean(bmi, na.rm = TRUE))
    )           

# check
yrbss_new |> 
  select(contains("bmi")) |> 
  tail()
# how does bmi_filled compare to bmi?
yrbss_new |> 
  select(bmi, bmi_filled) |> 
  get_summary_stats() |> 
  select(-iqr, -se, -ci) |> 
  gt()

```

### Example 2

-   We can also fill in missing values for character variables
-   Here we replace missingness in the `sex` column with `unknown`:

```{r}
class(yrbss_new$sex)
yrbss_new |> tabyl(sex)

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
    mutate(
      sexfilled = replace_na(sex, "unknown"))

# check
yrbss_new |> tabyl(sexfilled, sex) |> adorn_title()
```

### Example 3

-   What happens when we use `replace_na()` to fill in missing values for factor variables?

```{r}
class(yrbss_new$grade)
```


```{r}
#| error: true

yrbss_new <- yrbss_new |>
    mutate(
      gradefilled = replace_na(grade, "unknown"))
```

:::

# More data visualizations with `{ggplot2}`

## Boxplots with `geom_boxplot()`

-   Boxplots can be used to compare the distribution of a quantitative variable among categories.
    -   We need a categorical variable (here, our `x` axis)
    -   and a continuous numeric variable, shown here on our `y` axis.

```{r}
#| output-location: column

# creating a boxplot

ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = grade, 
      y = bmi) +
  geom_boxplot()

# Note that the grades are in the correct order now!
```

-   The main differences from the scatterplots we created earlier are the `geom` type and the variables plotted.

-   We can change the color similarly to scatterplots.

    -   However, we map to `fill` and not `color` if we want to fill in the box with color:

```{r}
#| output-location: column

# adding color
ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = grade, 
      y = bmi,
      fill = grade) +
  geom_boxplot()
```


## Faceting our boxplot with `facet_wrap()`

::: columns
::: {.column width="35%"}
-   One of the most powerful ways to change a visualization is by *faceting*.
    -   We can make multiple plots stratifying by a categorical variable.
-   To do this, we have to add the `facet_wrap()` function to our plot.
    -   We need to specify the variable to `facet_wrap` - `race4` by using the `vars()` function to specify it as a variable.
:::

::: {.column width="65%"}
```{r}
#| fig.height: 8

# facet_wrap:
ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = bmi, 
      y = grade,
      fill = grade) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(vars(factor(race4)))  #NEW!
```
:::
:::

-   *Don't try to facet on a continuous `numeric` variable - it won't work.*

-   Don't forget to look at the help documentation (e.g., `?facet_wrap`) to learn more about additional ways to customize your plots!

## Faceting our boxplot with `facet_grid()`

::: columns
::: {.column width="35%"}
-   Another way to facet our plots is with `facet_grid()`,
    -   which lets us select the rows and columns based on categorical variables:
:::

::: {.column width="65%"}
```{r}
#| fig.height: 8
#| fig.width: 12

ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  aes(x = bmi, 
      y = sex,
      fill = sex) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_grid(rows = vars(grade),  #NEW!
             cols = vars(race4))
```
:::
:::

## Mini-practice

Facet the boxplot below by `age` and `grade`, don't forget the `vars()`:

```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
# change eval: TRUE above

ggplot(yrbss_new) +
  
  aes(x = bmi, 
      y = sex,
      fill = sex) +
  
  geom_boxplot() + 
  
  facet_grid(______)
```



# Practice time! (part 7)

1.  Create a barplot of `race4`, faceted by `sex`.



# Useful keyboard shortcuts

[Full list of keyboard shortcuts](https://support.posit.co/hc/en-us/articles/200711853-Keyboard-Shortcuts){target="_blank"}

| action                              | mac                | windows/linux   |
|-------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Run code in qmd (or script)         | cmd + enter        | ctrl + enter    |
| `<-`                                | option + -         | alt + -         |
| interrupt currently running code    | esc                | esc             |
| keyboard shortcut help              | option + shift + k | alt + shift + k |



**Practice:** Try typing code below in your qmd (with shortcut) and evaluating it (with shortcut):

```{r}
#| eval: false
y <- 5
y
```

# Conclusions

![Artwork by \@allison_horst](image/horst_r_first_then.png){fig-align="center"}

## You WILL get frustrated while learning R!

From Garrett Grolemund's Prologue of his book *Hands-On Programming with R*[^1]:

[^1]: Grolemund, Garrett. 2014. Hands-on Programming with R. O’Reilly. [https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/](https://rstudio-education.github.io/hopr/){target="_blank"}

> As you learn to program, you are going to get frustrated. You are learning a new language, and it will take time to become fluent. But frustration is not just natural, it’s actually a positive sign that you should watch for. Frustration is your brain’s way of being lazy; it’s trying to get you to quit and go do something easy or fun. If you want to get physically fitter, you need to push your body even though it complains. If you want to get better at programming, you’ll need to push your brain. Recognize when you get frustrated and see it as a good thing: you’re now stretching yourself. Push yourself a little further every day, and you’ll soon be a confident programmer.

## Where are you in your R learning timeline?

![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/everything-else){target="_blank"}](image/horst_timeline_Rlearning.png){fig-align="center"}

# Resources

::: {.panel-tabset}
### R Resources

-   [R for Data Science](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/){target="_blank"} book by Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund
-   [R Programming for Health Data Science](https://niederhausen.github.io/BSTA_526_W26/){target="_blank"} (BSTA 526) course webpage

### Quarto Resources

-   [*Creating Professional Presentations and Websites using R/Quarto*](https://ohsu-octri-berd.github.io/Quarto_BERD_2025/){target="_blank"}
    -   OCTRI-BERD workshop from July 2025
-   [Official Quarto guide](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/){target="_blank"} 
    -   [Markdown basics](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html){target="_blank"} 
        -   Text formatting, headings, links, images, lists, tables, equations, diagrams, page breaks, keyboard shortcuts, and more!
    -   [Code blocks](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html#code-blocks){target="_blank"} 
        -   [Code chunk options:](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html#chunk-options){target="_blank"} 
-   Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel's [Quarto tip a day:](https://mine-cetinkaya-rundel.github.io/quarto-tip-a-day/){target="_blank"} 

:::

# Thank you!!
